title: Policestationrepjobsuk | PoliceStationRepUK

headings.text: Can I just apply for a Trainee Rep position?
Do I need a supervising solicitor before I can work?
Can I pay a solicitor to supervise me privately?
How much do police station reps earn?
Need a Police Station Rep in Kent?

content: HomePolice Station Rep Jobs UKPolice Station Rep Jobs UKFind work as an accredited police station representative across England & WalesImportant Career NoticeThe police station representative role is a regulated legal profession, not casual employment. You cannot "walk in" to this role without formal accreditation (PSRAS).The only realistic entry route is to first secure employment with a criminal defence law firm (e.g., as a paralegal or administrator) who will then sponsor and supervise your training. Independent qualification without an employer is virtually impossible due to supervision requirements.Types of Roles (For Accredited Reps)Freelance Cover WorkProvide overnight, weekend, and bank holiday cover for established firmsRequires Accreditation & ExperienceIn-House Rep PositionFull-time or part-time positions with criminal defence solicitorsAgency WorkWork through agencies that connect reps with firms needing coverSelf-Employed PracticeBuild your own client base working directly with multiple firmsDemand by RegionLondon & South EastVery HighKentHighView Kent reps →West MidlandsHighGreater ManchesterHighWest YorkshireModerateEssexModerateBecoming a Police Station RepresentativeThe Accreditation Process (PSRAS)To legally advise suspects, you must pass the Police Station Representative Accreditation Scheme (PSRAS).Warning: You cannot complete this qualification independently. You MUST have a supervising solicitor, which realistically means you need to be employed by a criminal defence firm first.1Training & RegistrationYou do not need a law degree. You must register with an assessment organisation (Cardiff University or Datalaw). The initial stage involves studying criminal law, PACE codes, and interview techniques.Cost: Approx. £300 - £500 for registration/written exam.2Portfolio StageYou must observe and then conduct police station attendances under the supervision of a qualified solicitor or rep. You compile these into a portfolio to demonstrate competence.Timeline: Realistically 12-18 months+. Requires employment for supervision.3Critical Incidents Test (CIT)The final hurdle is the CIT - an audio-based exam where you respond to role-play scenarios (e.g., conflicting instructions, silent interviews, ethical issues).Pass Rate: Varies, but preparation is essential.4Full AccreditationOnce passed, you receive your pin number from the Legal Aid Agency (LAA) and can advise clients unsupervised. You can now work as a freelancer or employee.View Full Step-by-Step Guide →Total Investment & Career GrowthExpect to spend £1,500 - £2,500 on training (often firm-funded). This qualification is your gateway to becoming a Duty Solicitor or even a Criminal Solicitor via the SQE route without a training contract.Read our complete guide on costs, exemptions, and career pathways →Frequently Asked QuestionsCan I just apply for a Trainee Rep position?Direct "Trainee Rep" vacancies are rare. Firms rarely advertise for someone just to train them. The realistic route is to apply for Paralegal, Legal Assistant, or Administrator roles at criminal defence firms first. Get your foot in the door, prove you are reliable, and then ask to start the accreditation process.Do I need a supervising solicitor before I can work?Yes. You cannot attend police stations (even for training) without being registered with the Legal Aid Agency under a supervising solicitor's oversight. This is why finding employment with a firm is the essential first step.Can I pay a solicitor to supervise me privately?This is technically possible but extremely rare and expensive (often £3,000+). Most solicitors will not accept the liability of supervising a non-employee. We strongly recommend securing employment where supervision is usually provided for free.How much do police station reps earn?Earnings depend heavily on whether you are employed or freelance:Employed: Typical salaries range from £24,000 to £32,000 per year[1].Freelance: A busy freelancer working nights/weekends can gross £40,000 - £60,000+[2], but income is variable and expenses are your own.Ready to Find Rep Work?Join our directory and connect with criminal defence firms looking for reliable police station representatives.Register as a RepJoin WhatsApp GroupReferences & NotesEmployed Salaries: Based on average salary data for "Police Station Representative" on Glassdoor (approx. £26k) and analysis of live job postings on Indeed/TotalJobs (range £24k-£32k) as of late 2024.Freelance Earnings: Estimated based on standard freelance rates (approx. 50-60% of fixed fee) and high-volume caseloads (10-15+ cases/week). Actual income varies significantly by region, "unsocial hours" uplift availability, and billable travel.PSRAS Regulation: The Police Station Representative Accreditation Scheme is governed by the Solicitors Regulation Authority (SRA) and administered by authorized assessment organizations.Portfolio Timeline: Completion time depends heavily on the candidate's ability to secure supervised attendance opportunities with a criminal defence firm.Revised by Robert Cashman on 28th November 2025
